nodes.text:
Alan Turing was born in London, England in 1912. He studied at King's College, Cambridge and later earned his PhD from Princeton University.

During World War II, Turing worked at Bletchley Park, where he played a crucial role in breaking the German Enigma code. He developed the Bombe, an electromechanical device used to decipher Enigma-encrypted messages.

ode. He developed the Bombe, an electromechanical device used to decipher Enigma-encrypted messages.

After the war, Turing worked at the National Physical Laboratory and later at the University of Manchester. He is widely considered the father of theoretical computer science and artificial intelligence. His 1950 paper "Computing Machinery and Intelligence" introduced the Turing Test.

cial intelligence. His 1950 paper "Computing Machinery and Intelligence" introduced the Turing Test.

Turing also made significant contributions to mathematical biology, publishing work on morphogenesis. He died in 1954 in Wilmslow, England.

Marie Curie was born in Warsaw, Poland in 1867. She is best known for her pioneering research on radioactivity. She discovered the elements polonium and radium.

Curie worked at the University of Paris, where she became the first female professor. In 1903, she received the Nobel Prize in Physics along with Pierre Curie and Henri Becquerel for their work on radioactivity.

Nobel Prize in Physics along with Pierre Curie and Henri Becquerel for their work on radioactivity.

In 1911, she won a second Nobel Prize, this time in Chemistry, for her discovery of radium and polonium. She founded the Curie Institutes in Paris and Warsaw, which remain major medical research centers today.

Marie Curie died in 1934 in Passy, France from aplastic anemia, likely caused by her long-term exposure to radiation.

nodes.name: Alan Turing
nodes.description: Alan Turing was a pioneering British mathematician, logician, and computer scientist, born in London in 1912. He is considered the father of theoretical computer science and artificial intelligence, having made foundational contributions to both fields as well as to mathematical biology. During World War II, he worked at Bletchley Park, where he developed the Bombe and played a crucial role in breaking the Enigma code. After the war, he continued his influential work at the National Physical Laboratory and the University of Manchester.

nodes.name: Turing Test
nodes.description: The Turing Test is a test of a machine's ability to exhibit intelligent behavior equivalent to, or indistinguishable from, that of a human. It was introduced by Alan Turing in his 1950 paper, "Computing Machinery and Intelligence."
1950 is the year in which Alan Turing published his influential and seminal paper, "Computing Machinery and Intelligence."

nodes.description: A field of computer science focused on creating machines capable of intelligent behavior, to which Alan Turing made foundational contributions.

nodes.name: Marie Curie
nodes.description: Marie Curie was a pioneering physicist and chemist, born in Warsaw, Poland, in 1867. She is renowned for her groundbreaking research on radioactivity, which led to the discovery of the elements polonium and radium. Her exceptional contributions to science earned her Nobel Prizes in both Physics and Chemistry. In addition to her research, she founded the Curie Institutes, which continue to be important centers for medical research. Marie Curie died from radiation-induced aplastic anemia, a condition resulting from her prolonged exposure to radioactive materials during her scientific work.

nodes.description: Warsaw is the capital city of Poland, notable as the birthplace of Marie Curie and the location of one of the Curie Institutes that she founded.

The year Marie Curie was born.

nodes.name: radioactivity
nodes.description: Radioactivity is the phenomenon of spontaneous emission of radiation. Pioneering research on this phenomenon was conducted by Marie Curie, who, along with Pierre Curie and Henri Becquerel, was recognized for this work.

nodes.name: polonium
nodes.description: Polonium is a radioactive chemical element discovered by Marie Curie, who named it after her native Poland.

nodes.name: radium
nodes.description: Radium is a radioactive chemical element discovered by Marie Curie, a groundbreaking achievement that contributed to her winning a second Nobel Prize.

nodes.name: University of Paris

nodes.name: Nobel Prize in Physics
nodes.description: The Nobel Prize in Physics is a prestigious award that was received jointly by Marie Curie, Pierre Curie, and Henri Becquerel in 1903. They were recognized for their collective work on radioactivity.
1903 was the year Marie Curie, Pierre Curie, and Henri Becquerel were jointly awarded the Nobel Prize in Physics for their groundbreaking work on radioactivity.

nodes.name: Pierre Curie
nodes.description: Pierre Curie was a physicist and the husband of Marie Curie. He shared the 1903 Nobel Prize in Physics with Marie Curie and Henri Becquerel for their work on radioactivity.

nodes.name: Henri Becquerel
nodes.description: Henri Becquerel was a physicist who shared the 1903 Nobel Prize in Physics with Marie and Pierre Curie for their collective work on radioactivity, including his discovery of spontaneous radioactivity.

nodes.description: Major medical research centers founded by Marie Curie in Paris and Warsaw that remain operational today.

entities.name: Alan Turing
entities.description: Alan Turing was a pioneering British mathematician, logician, and computer scientist, born in London in 1912. He is considered the father of theoretical computer science and artificial intelligence, having made foundational contributions to both fields as well as to mathematical biology. During World War II, he worked at Bletchley Park, where he developed the Bombe and played a crucial role in breaking the Enigma code. After the war, he continued his influential work at the National Physical Laboratory and the University of Manchester.

entities.name: Marie Curie
entities.description: Marie Curie was a pioneering physicist and chemist, born in Warsaw, Poland, in 1867. She is renowned for her groundbreaking research on radioactivity, which led to the discovery of the elements polonium and radium. Her exceptional contributions to science earned her Nobel Prizes in both Physics and Chemistry. In addition to her research, she founded the Curie Institutes, which continue to be important centers for medical research. Marie Curie died from radiation-induced aplastic anemia, a condition resulting from her prolonged exposure to radioactive materials during her scientific work.
